---
title: "Detectability level curves for gene-set enrichment"
subtitle: "Expected-LRT iso-detectability over set size, enrichment, and per-gene signal"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    fig-format: png
engine: knitr
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# knitr engine so the doc is polyglot (R + Python). The analysis chunks are Python,
# driven through reticulate against the repo-root uv venv. This file lives in
# twogroup_experiments/analysis/, so `proj_root` is the twogroup_experiments project
# root. The venv is one level up (the repo root), shared across the whole
# gibss-experiments repo. This notebook needs only numpy/scipy/matplotlib - no
# results/, no manifest, no gibss imports.
proj_root <- normalizePath(file.path(dirname(knitr::current_input(dir = TRUE)), ".."))
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = proj_root)
Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = file.path(proj_root, "..", ".venv", "bin", "python"))
library(reticulate)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

## Overview

For gene-set enrichment under the two-group model there are three knobs we control:
the **gene-set size** $m$, the **enrichment log-odds** $b$, and the **per-gene
signal** of the effect distribution $f_1$ (a location shift $\mu$, or a scale
inflation $\sigma$). The background membership log-odds is fixed at $b_0 = -2$.

We plot the *detectability landscape*

$$ f(m, b, \text{signal}) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\big[\,\mathrm{LRT}\ \text{for enrichment of the causal set}\,\big], $$

the expected likelihood-ratio-test statistic for detecting that the set is enriched. As
derived below, this is exactly $f = 2m\,\mathrm{KL}(q_\ast \Vert q_0) + c$ - **linear in
the set size** $m$ - so the whole picture is one **per-gene** landscape $2\,\mathrm{KL}$
over $(b, \text{signal})$, and set size simply scales it. The message: a larger set
reaches the same detectability at a **smaller** $b$ and a **smaller** per-gene signal;
large sets are detectable even when each gene's effect is weak, while small sets need a
strong per-gene effect to compensate.

### The model and the statistic

Two-group model, per gene $i$, with a single gene-set indicator $x_i \in \{0,1\}$
($1$ iff gene $i$ is in the causal set $S$, $|S| = m$):

- Latent membership $\gamma_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(\pi_i)$ with
  $\pi_i = \sigma(b_0 + b\,x_i)$, $\sigma(t) = 1/(1+e^{-t})$, $b_0 = -2$ fixed.
- Effect $\theta_i \mid \gamma_i{=}0 \sim \delta_0$ and
  $\theta_i \mid \gamma_i{=}1 \sim f_1$, with
  $f_1 = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau_0^2)$, $\tau_0 = 0.01$ (**loc**), or
  $f_1 = \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (**scale**).
- Observed z-score $z_i = \theta_i + \varepsilon_i$, $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

So the observed-data mixture components the oracle knows are $g_0(z) = \mathcal{N}(z;0,1)$
and $g_1(z) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu, \tau_0^2 + 1)$ (loc) or $g_1(z) = \mathcal{N}(z; 0, \sigma^2 + 1)$
(scale). The per-gene marginal log-likelihood at membership log-odds $\eta$ is
$\ell_i(\eta) = \log\!\big[(1 - \sigma(\eta))\,g_0(z_i) + \sigma(\eta)\,g_1(z_i)\big]$,
and the statistic (testing $H_0: b = 0$ against $H_1: b$ free, $b_0$ fixed in both) is

$$ \mathrm{LRT}(z_{1:m}) = 2\Big[\max_{b}\ \textstyle\sum_i \ell_i(b_0 + b\,x_i)\ -\ \sum_i \ell_i(b_0)\Big]. $$

**Only the in-set genes matter.** Because $b_0$ is fixed, every out-of-set gene has
$x_i = 0$, so its term is identical under $H_0$ and $H_1$ and cancels. The statistic
depends only on the $m$ genes in the set - no design matrix, no total gene count, no
competitor sets. (Profiling $b_0$ instead would let the out-set genes re-enter weakly
through $\hat b_0$ and reintroduce an $n$ dependence; we fix $b_0$ for clarity.)

### The expected LRT is closed-form (no Monte-Carlo)

Take the expectation of the statistic over $z_{1:m}$ drawn at the true parameters
$(b_0, b^\ast)$, and write the true and null per-gene z-marginals as the two-component
mixtures
$$ q_\ast(z) = (1-p_\ast)\,g_0(z) + p_\ast\,g_1(z), \qquad q_0(z) = (1-p_0)\,g_0(z) + p_0\,g_1(z), $$
with $p_\ast = \sigma(b_0 + b^\ast)$ and $p_0 = \sigma(b_0)$. Two facts collapse the
expectation:

- The **null term** is exactly linear in the data:
  $\mathbb{E}\big[\sum_i \ell_i(b_0)\big] = m\,\mathbb{E}_{z\sim q_\ast}[\log q_0(z)]$.
- The model is **well-specified** (the data really are drawn with membership rate
  $p_\ast$), so the population log-likelihood $\bar\ell(b) = \mathbb{E}_{z\sim q_\ast}[\log q_b(z)]$
  is maximized at $b = b^\ast$, and by standard MLE asymptotics
  $\mathbb{E}\big[\max_b \sum_i \ell_i(b_0+b)\big] = m\,\bar\ell(b^\ast) + \tfrac12\,c$
  with $c\to 1$ (the LRT has one degree of freedom, so the profile max overshoots its
  population value by $\tfrac12$).

Subtracting, $\bar\ell(b^\ast) - \mathbb{E}_{q_\ast}[\log q_0] = \mathrm{KL}(q_\ast \Vert q_0)$, so

$$ f(m, b, \text{signal}) \;=\; 2m\,\mathrm{KL}\!\big(q_\ast \Vert q_0\big) \;+\; c, \qquad c \approx 1. $$

The per-gene $\mathrm{KL}$ is a single one-dimensional integral with a closed-form
integrand, so the whole landscape is exact quadrature - no simulation, no Monte-Carlo
noise, instant. Two consequences we use below: the dependence on set size $m$ is
**exactly linear**, so we plot the single per-gene field $2\,\mathrm{KL}$ and let size
scale it; and the additive $c \approx 1$ is the null offset ($f \to 1$ as $b^\ast \to 0$,
the expected $\chi^2_1$). We carry $c$ explicitly (the detectability target below solves
$2m\,\mathrm{KL} + c = 25$), rather than dropping it. We verified $2m\,\mathrm{KL} + 1$
against a direct Monte-Carlo of the profiled LRT: they agree to within Monte-Carlo error
(the residual is the $c$ offset, $0.9$-$1.1$; see the sanity table).

```{python}
#| label: core
#| code-summary: "Expected-LRT compute (closed-form KL quadrature)"
import numpy as np

B0 = -2.0                      # fixed background membership log-odds
TAU0 = 0.01                    # loc-scenario per-gene effect sd (f1 = N(mu, tau0^2))
C_OFFSET = 1.0                 # profile-max overshoot = LRT degrees of freedom (2 * 1/2)
_ZGRID = np.linspace(-15.0, 20.0, 4000)             # z-quadrature grid for the per-gene KL
_DZ = _ZGRID[1] - _ZGRID[0]


def _sigmoid(t):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-t))


def _npdf(z, mean, sd):
    return np.exp(-0.5 * ((z - mean) / sd) ** 2) / (sd * np.sqrt(2.0 * np.pi))


def kl_field(scenario, b_axis, sig_axis, b0=B0):
    """Per-gene KL(q* || q0) over (b_axis on x, sig_axis on y). Exact 1-D quadrature.

    q_p(z) = (1-p) g0(z) + p g1(z) is the observed z-marginal at membership rate p;
    q* uses p* = sigmoid(b0 + b) (row varies over b_axis), q0 uses p0 = sigmoid(b0). g1
    is N(mu, tau0^2+1) for scenario "loc" (sig_axis = mu) or N(0, sigma^2+1) for "scale"
    (sig_axis = sigma). Returns KL[iy, ib]; the expected LRT for set size m is then
    2 m KL + C_OFFSET. b0 is the background membership log-odds (default B0 = -2).
    """
    g0 = _npdf(_ZGRID, 0.0, 1.0)
    p0 = _sigmoid(b0)
    p_star = _sigmoid(b0 + b_axis)                  # (nb,)
    KL = np.empty((len(sig_axis), len(b_axis)))
    for iy, s in enumerate(sig_axis):
        g1_mean, g1_sd = (s, np.sqrt(TAU0 ** 2 + 1.0)) if scenario == "loc" else (0.0, np.sqrt(s ** 2 + 1.0))
        g1 = _npdf(_ZGRID, g1_mean, g1_sd)
        q0 = (1.0 - p0) * g0 + p0 * g1                              # (nz,)
        q_star = (1.0 - p_star)[:, None] * g0[None, :] + p_star[:, None] * g1[None, :]  # (nb, nz)
        KL[iy] = np.sum(q_star * (np.log(q_star) - np.log(q0)[None, :]), axis=1) * _DZ
    return KL


def expected_lrt(m, KL):
    """Set-level expected LRT F = 2 m KL + C_OFFSET, from the per-gene KL field."""
    return 2.0 * m * KL + C_OFFSET


LRT_TARGET = 25.0                                   # set-level difficulty target (expected LRT)
print(f"per-gene expected-LRT contribution = 2 KL(q*||q0); set F = 2 m KL + {C_OFFSET}; "
      f"b0={B0} fixed, tau0={TAU0}")
```

## The per-observation detectability landscape

Because set size enters only as the linear multiplier $2m$, one per-gene field carries
the whole story. We plot the per-gene expected-LRT contribution
$2\,\mathrm{KL}(q_\ast \Vert q_0)$ over the two signal types: a **location** shift
$f_1 = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau_0^2)$, $\tau_0 = 0.01$ (a causal gene's z is
$\mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau_0^2 + 1)$), and a **scale** inflation $f_1 = \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$
(z is $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 + 1)$, $\mathbb{E}[z^2] = \sigma^2 + 1$). To read a set-level
number off the map, multiply by the set size: a set of $m$ genes has expected
$\mathrm{LRT} = m \cdot 2\,\mathrm{KL} + 1$.

The dashed lines make that reading concrete. Each is an **iso-detectability curve for a
fixed set size**: along the line labelled $m$, the parameters $(b, \text{signal})$ give a
size-$m$ set an expected $\mathrm{LRT} \approx 25$ - i.e. the locus $2\,\mathrm{KL} \approx
24/m$. So the plot reads two ways: the colour is the per-gene evidence, and picking any point
on the $m$-line is a recipe for a just-detectable size-$m$ set.

```{python}
#| label: compute
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Boxes: loc (b,mu) in [0,4]x[0,4], scale (b,sigma) in [0,4]x[0,6]. These are both the
# figure axis limits and the box the design points below are spread over.
b_axis_loc = np.linspace(0.0, 4.0, 160)
mu_axis = np.linspace(0.0, 4.0, 160)
kl_loc = kl_field("loc", b_axis_loc, mu_axis)

b_axis_scale = np.linspace(0.0, 4.0, 160)
sigma_axis = np.linspace(0.0, 6.0, 160)
kl_scale = kl_field("scale", b_axis_scale, sigma_axis)

perobs_loc = 2.0 * kl_loc                           # per-gene expected-LRT contribution
perobs_scale = 2.0 * kl_scale
print("shapes:", perobs_loc.shape, perobs_scale.shape,
      "| per-gene 2*KL range loc:", (round(perobs_loc.min(), 3), round(perobs_loc.max(), 2)),
      "scale:", (round(perobs_scale.min(), 3), round(perobs_scale.max(), 2)))
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-perobs
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 5.2
#| fig-cap: "Per-gene expected-LRT contribution 2*KL(q*||q0) over enrichment b and per-gene signal, for location (mu) and scale (sigma). Colour = the per-observation value; a set of m genes has expected LRT = m*(this) + 1. Each dashed line is an iso-detectability locus: along it, a set of the labelled size m reaches an expected LRT ~ 25. b0=-2 fixed, in-set genes only."
# Iso-detectability set sizes: the dashed line labelled m is the locus where a set of m
# genes reaches the LRT_TARGET, i.e. 2 m KL + C_OFFSET = LRT_TARGET  <=>  2 KL = 24/m.
SIZE_REFS = [50, 100, 200, 400]


def _panel(ax, B, Y, perobs, ylabel, title, vmax):
    cf = ax.contourf(B, Y, np.clip(perobs, 0, vmax), levels=np.linspace(0, vmax, 21), cmap="Blues")
    size_levels = sorted((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / m for m in SIZE_REFS)   # 2*KL = 24/m
    cl = ax.contour(B, Y, perobs, levels=size_levels, colors="#08306b",
                    linestyles="--", linewidths=1.3)
    lbl = {lv: f"m={int(round((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / lv))}" for lv in size_levels}
    ax.clabel(cl, fmt=lbl, fontsize=8)
    ax.set(xlabel="enrichment log-odds  $b$", ylabel=ylabel, title=title)
    ax.set_box_aspect(1)                             # square panel
    return cf

fig, (axL, axS) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5.2))
BB, YY = np.meshgrid(b_axis_loc, mu_axis)
BBs, SS = np.meshgrid(b_axis_scale, sigma_axis)
vmax = max(float(np.percentile(perobs_loc, 99.5)), float(np.percentile(perobs_scale, 99.5)))
_panel(axL, BB, YY, perobs_loc, "per-gene shift  $\\mu$", "Location", vmax)
cfS = _panel(axS, BBs, SS, perobs_scale, "per-gene effect sd  $\\sigma$", "Scale", vmax)
cb = fig.colorbar(cfS, ax=[axL, axS], label="per-gene expected LRT  $2\\,\\mathrm{KL}$", shrink=0.85)
cb.set_ticks(np.arange(0.0, vmax + 1e-9, 0.5))       # clean round ticks, shared scale
# one shared note on what the dashed lines mean
axL.legend([plt.Line2D([], [], color="#08306b", ls="--", lw=1.3)],
           ["set size $m$ reaching\nexpected $\\mathrm{LRT}\\approx25$"],
           loc="lower right", fontsize=7, frameon=True, framealpha=0.9)
fig
```

Both panels climb toward the top-right (strong enrichment and strong per-gene signal), and
the $m$-lines nest toward the origin: a large set ($m = 400$) is just-detectable at a
per-gene signal well inside where a small set ($m = 50$) still is not. Comparing the two
panels at a fixed $b$, the location field reaches any given per-gene contribution at a
smaller signal than the scale field does - a directed mean shift is stronger, gene-for-gene,
than sign-agnostic variance inflation (e.g. at $b = 2$: $2\,\mathrm{KL} = 0.43$ at $\mu = 2$
vs $0.20$ at $\sigma = 2$, both a causal $\mathbb{E}[z^2] = 5$).

## Effect of the background rate $b_0$

The panels above fix $b_0 = -2$. The background rate sets how common set membership is for
a non-enriched gene ($p_0 = \sigma(b_0)$: $0.02, 0.05, 0.12, 0.27$ at $b_0 = -4, -3, -2, -1$)
and the null marginal $q_0$ the test contrasts against. We recompute the per-gene landscape
across $b_0 \in \{-4, -3, -2, -1\}$, holding everything else fixed, once for each signal type.

```{python}
#| label: fig-b0-loc
#| fig-width: 18
#| fig-height: 4.4
#| fig-cap: "Location per-gene expected-LRT 2*KL over (b, mu) at four background rates b0 = -4, -3, -2, -1 (shared colour scale). Dashed lines = iso-detectability set sizes (a size-m set reaches expected LRT ~ 25 along the line). b0 shifts both the enrichment mapping p*=sigmoid(b0+b) and the null marginal."
B0_GRID = [-4, -3, -2, -1]


def _b0_row(scenario, b_axis, sig_axis, ylabel, cmap):
    fields = [2.0 * kl_field(scenario, b_axis, sig_axis, b0=b0) for b0 in B0_GRID]
    vmax = max(float(np.percentile(f, 99.5)) for f in fields)
    levels = np.linspace(0.0, vmax, 21)
    size_levels = sorted((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / m for m in SIZE_REFS)
    lbl = {lv: f"m={int(round((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / lv))}" for lv in size_levels}
    B, Y = np.meshgrid(b_axis, sig_axis)
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(16, 4.6), sharex=True, sharey=True)
    for ax, b0, perobs in zip(axes, B0_GRID, fields):
        cf = ax.contourf(B, Y, np.clip(perobs, 0, vmax), levels=levels, cmap=cmap)
        cl = ax.contour(B, Y, perobs, levels=size_levels, colors="#08306b",
                        linestyles="--", linewidths=1.1)
        ax.clabel(cl, fmt=lbl, fontsize=7)
        ax.set(title=f"$b_0 = {b0}$  ($p_0={_sigmoid(b0):.2f}$)", xlabel="enrichment log-odds  $b$")
        ax.set_box_aspect(1)                         # square panels
    axes[0].set_ylabel(ylabel)
    cb = fig.colorbar(cf, ax=axes, label="per-gene expected LRT  $2\\,\\mathrm{KL}$", shrink=0.85)
    cb.set_ticks(np.arange(0.0, vmax + 1e-9, 0.5))
    return fig


_b0_row("loc", b_axis_loc, mu_axis, "per-gene shift  $\\mu$", "Blues")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-b0-scale
#| fig-width: 18
#| fig-height: 4.4
#| fig-cap: "Scale per-gene expected-LRT 2*KL over (b, sigma) at four background rates b0 = -4, -3, -2, -1 (shared colour scale). Dashed lines = iso-detectability set sizes (a size-m set reaches expected LRT ~ 25 along the line)."
_b0_row("scale", b_axis_scale, sigma_axis, "per-gene effect sd  $\\sigma$", "Blues")
```

Per-gene detectability is **non-monotonic** in $b_0$. At a very low baseline ($b_0 = -4$,
$p_0 = 0.02$) even an enriched set is almost all background - the enriched rate
$p_\ast = \sigma(b_0 + b)$ is still tiny - so $q_\ast \approx q_0$ and the per-gene contrast
is small. Raising $b_0$ lifts $p_\ast$, the enriched set carries visibly more signal genes,
and the per-gene $2\,\mathrm{KL}$ **grows** while the iso-detectability $m$-lines pull toward
the origin - across the whole $b_0 = -4 \to -1$ range shown (e.g. at $b = 2, \mu = 1.5$:
$2\,\mathrm{KL} = 0.04, 0.13, 0.28, 0.34$). The contrast peaks at an intermediate baseline
(here near $b_0 \approx -1$); pushed further toward $b_0 = 0$ the null background itself
fills with signal genes ($p_0 = 0.5$) and the contrast collapses again (the same point drops
to $0.24$ at $b_0 = 0$). So there is a most-detectable background rate, neither empty nor
half-full.

## Proposed simulation design points

To turn the landscape into a simulation plan we place points on the iso-detectability
curves (back at $b_0 = -2$). For each set size $m \in \{50, 100, 200, 400\}$ the curve is the
locus of expected $\mathrm{LRT} = 25$ - the dashed $m$-line. We pick **three points per
curve** spanning the enrichment/signal trade-off with a **normalized arc-length** rule:
scale each axis by its box width (loc $(b,\mu) \in [0,4]\times[0,4]$, scale
$(b,\sigma) \in [0,4]\times[0,6]$), trace the in-box curve, and sample at arc-length
$0, \tfrac12, 1$. The two endpoints are the box corners of the curve - **enrichment-heavy**
(most $b$, least signal) and **signal-heavy** (most signal, least $b$) - and the midpoint is
the balanced knee. Normalizing by the box first is what makes "evenly spaced along the curve"
well-defined: without it, arc length would depend on the arbitrary $b$-vs-signal unit scaling.
All 24 points share the same expected $\mathrm{LRT}$, so difficulty is matched across the
whole design; what varies is *how* the detectability is sourced.

```{python}
#| label: design-compute
DESIGN_SIZES = [50, 100, 200, 400]
REGIME_ORDER = ["enrichment-heavy", "balanced", "signal-heavy"]


def tradeoff_points(perobs, b_axis, sig_axis, m):
    """Three (b, signal) points on the size-m iso-detectability curve, by normalized arc
    length. Endpoints = the enrichment-heavy / signal-heavy box corners of the curve,
    midpoint = the balanced knee. Returns dicts sorted enrichment-heavy -> signal-heavy.
    """
    level = (LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / m          # 2*KL contour giving expected LRT = target
    B, Y = np.meshgrid(b_axis, sig_axis)
    _f = plt.figure()
    segs = plt.contour(B, Y, perobs, levels=[level]).allsegs[0]  # curve polyline(s)
    plt.close(_f)
    if not segs:
        return []
    seg = max(segs, key=len)                     # longest segment = the main curve
    bw, sw = b_axis[-1] - b_axis[0], sig_axis[-1] - sig_axis[0]
    xn, yn = seg[:, 0] / bw, seg[:, 1] / sw      # normalize each axis by its box width
    d = np.concatenate([[0.0], np.cumsum(np.hypot(np.diff(xn), np.diff(yn)))])
    d /= d[-1]
    pts = [(float(np.interp(fr, d, seg[:, 0])), float(np.interp(fr, d, seg[:, 1])))
           for fr in (0.0, 0.5, 1.0)]
    pts.sort(key=lambda p: p[1])                 # ascending signal: enrich-heavy -> signal-heavy
    return [{"m": m, "regime": r, "b": b, "signal": s} for r, (b, s) in zip(REGIME_ORDER, pts)]


design_loc = [dict(p, scenario="loc") for m in DESIGN_SIZES
              for p in tradeoff_points(perobs_loc, b_axis_loc, mu_axis, m)]
design_scale = [dict(p, scenario="scale") for m in DESIGN_SIZES
                for p in tradeoff_points(perobs_scale, b_axis_scale, sigma_axis, m)]
print(f"{len(design_loc)} loc + {len(design_scale)} scale design points (target LRT={LRT_TARGET:g})")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-design
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 5.6
#| fig-cap: "The two per-observation panels (shared Blues scale) with the proposed design points on each iso-detectability curve m = 50, 100, 200, 400. Marker shape/colour = trade-off regime (enrichment-heavy circle, balanced diamond, signal-heavy triangle). Every point sits on its m-line, so all share expected LRT = 25 at b0 = -2."
REGIME_MARKER = {"enrichment-heavy": "o", "balanced": "D", "signal-heavy": "^"}
REGIME_COLOR = {"enrichment-heavy": "#E69F00", "balanced": "#CC79A7", "signal-heavy": "#009E73"}

fig, (axL, axS) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5.6))
vmax = max(float(np.percentile(perobs_loc, 99.5)), float(np.percentile(perobs_scale, 99.5)))
panels = [(axL, perobs_loc, b_axis_loc, mu_axis, "per-gene shift  $\\mu$", "Location", design_loc),
          (axS, perobs_scale, b_axis_scale, sigma_axis, "per-gene effect sd  $\\sigma$", "Scale", design_scale)]
for ax, perobs, b_axis, sig_axis, ylabel, title, design in panels:
    B, Y = np.meshgrid(b_axis, sig_axis)
    cf = ax.contourf(B, Y, np.clip(perobs, 0, vmax), levels=np.linspace(0, vmax, 21), cmap="Blues")
    size_levels = sorted((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / m for m in SIZE_REFS)
    cl = ax.contour(B, Y, perobs, levels=size_levels, colors="#08306b", linestyles="--", linewidths=1.0)
    ax.clabel(cl, fmt={lv: f"m={int(round((LRT_TARGET - C_OFFSET) / lv))}" for lv in size_levels}, fontsize=7)
    for p in design:
        ax.plot(p["b"], p["signal"], marker=REGIME_MARKER[p["regime"]], ms=7,
                mfc=REGIME_COLOR[p["regime"]], mec="white", mew=0.7, ls="none", zorder=5)
    ax.set(xlabel="enrichment log-odds  $b$", ylabel=ylabel, title=title)
    ax.set_box_aspect(1)
cb = fig.colorbar(cf, ax=[axL, axS], label="per-gene expected LRT  $2\\,\\mathrm{KL}$", shrink=0.85)
cb.set_ticks(np.arange(0.0, vmax + 1e-9, 0.5))
handles = [plt.Line2D([], [], marker=REGIME_MARKER[r], color=REGIME_COLOR[r], mec="white",
                      ls="none", ms=8, label=r) for r in REGIME_ORDER]
axL.legend(handles=handles, loc="upper right", fontsize=7, frameon=True, framealpha=0.9)
fig
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-design
#| output: asis
def _kl_pt(scenario, b, s):
    return float(kl_field(scenario, np.array([b]), np.array([s]))[0, 0])

# f1 = N(mu, sigma^2): loc varies mu at sigma=tau0; scale varies sigma at mu=0.
_hdr = ["scenario", "size m", "regime", "b", "mu", "sigma", "p* = sigmoid(b0+b)", "E[active] = m p*", "E[LRT]"]
_rows = [f"Proposed difficulty-matched design points ($b_0 = {B0:g}$, target expected LRT $= {LRT_TARGET:g}$):\n",
         "| " + " | ".join(_hdr) + " |", "| " + " | ".join("---" for _ in _hdr) + " |"]
for _p in design_loc + design_scale:
    _b, _s, _m, _sc = _p["b"], _p["signal"], _p["m"], _p["scenario"]
    _mu, _sigma = (_s, TAU0) if _sc == "loc" else (0.0, _s)   # f1 = N(mu, sigma^2)
    _ps = _sigmoid(B0 + _b)
    _lrt = 2.0 * _m * _kl_pt(_sc, _b, _s) + C_OFFSET
    _rows.append("| " + " | ".join([_sc, str(_m), _p["regime"], f"{_b:.2f}", f"{_mu:.2f}",
                                    f"{_sigma:.2f}", f"{_ps:.2f}", f"{_m * _ps:.0f}", f"{_lrt:.1f}"]) + " |")
print("\n".join(_rows))
```

**Columns.** The causal effect distribution is $f_1 = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ in every
cell. *scenario* - loc (mean shift: $\mu$ varies, $\sigma = \tau_0 = 0.01$) or scale (variance
inflation: $\mu = 0$, $\sigma$ varies); *size m* - causal-set size; *regime* - which trade-off
point on the curve; *b* - enrichment log-odds ($b_0 = -2$ fixed); *mu*, *sigma* - the $f_1$
mean and sd; *p\** $= \sigma(b_0 + b)$ - membership rate inside the causal set; *E[active]*
$= m\,p_\ast$ - expected number of active genes planted in the set; *E[LRT]* - the expected
LRT the closed form assigns the point (matched to 25 by construction, small quadrature wiggle
aside).

## Sanity checks

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sanity
#| output: asis
def _md(rows, hdr):
    out = ["| " + " | ".join(hdr) + " |", "| " + " | ".join("---" for _ in hdr) + " |"]
    for r in rows:
        out.append("| " + " | ".join(str(v) for v in r) + " |")
    return "\n".join(out)

# 1. Monotone in b (fixed mu, medium set), in mu (fixed b), and in m.
_iy2 = int(np.argmin(np.abs(mu_axis - 1.5)))
_ib2 = int(np.argmin(np.abs(b_axis_loc - 2.0)))
mono_b = np.all(np.diff(perobs_loc[_iy2, :]) > 0.0)              # exact quadrature: strict
mono_mu = np.all(np.diff(perobs_loc[:, _ib2]) > 0.0)
# set size is a positive multiplier, so per-gene monotonicity ⇒ set-level monotonicity in m
mono_m = (expected_lrt(50, perobs_loc[_iy2, _ib2] / 2) <
          expected_lrt(300, perobs_loc[_iy2, _ib2] / 2))

# 2. Reference set-level point f(m=50, b=3, mu=2) ~ 46 (plan calibrated m=45 -> 46.5).
_kl_ref = float(np.interp(3.0, b_axis_loc, kl_loc[int(np.argmin(np.abs(mu_axis - 2.0)))]))
_ref = expected_lrt(50, _kl_ref)

# 3. LRT=25 target reached at smaller b for a large set than a small one, on the mu=1.5 row.
def _min_b_reaching(m, iy, target=LRT_TARGET):
    F = expected_lrt(m, kl_loc[iy])
    hit = np.where(F >= target)[0]
    return float(b_axis_loc[hit[0]]) if len(hit) else float("nan")
_bmin_small = _min_b_reaching(50, _iy2)
_bmin_large = _min_b_reaching(300, _iy2)

# 4. Scale is harder per gene: at a matched per-gene energy (loc mu=2 vs scale sigma=2,
# both a causal E[z^2]=5) and matched b, the location contribution exceeds the scale one.
# Compare at the signal value, NOT the raw grid index - the two y-axes cover different ranges.
_iy_loc2 = int(np.argmin(np.abs(mu_axis - 2.0)))
_iy_sca2 = int(np.argmin(np.abs(sigma_axis - 2.0)))
_loc_at2 = perobs_loc[_iy_loc2, _ib2]
_sca_at2 = perobs_scale[_iy_sca2, _ib2]
_scale_harder = _loc_at2 > _sca_at2

# 5. Monte-Carlo cross-check of the closed form at one point: simulate the profiled LRT
# directly and confirm it matches 2 m KL + C_OFFSET. This is the only place we draw z.
from scipy.optimize import minimize_scalar
from scipy.stats import norm


def _mc_lrt(m, b_true, mu, tau0=TAU0, n_rep=4000, seed=0):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    sd1 = np.sqrt(tau0 ** 2 + 1.0)
    p = _sigmoid(B0 + b_true)
    out = np.empty(n_rep)
    for r in range(n_rep):
        gam = rng.binomial(1, p, m)
        z = np.where(gam == 1, rng.normal(mu, sd1, m), rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, m))
        d0, d1 = norm.pdf(z, 0.0, 1.0), norm.pdf(z, mu, sd1)
        ll0 = np.sum(np.log((1 - _sigmoid(B0)) * d0 + _sigmoid(B0) * d1))
        neg = lambda b: -np.sum(np.log((1 - _sigmoid(B0 + b)) * d0 + _sigmoid(B0 + b) * d1))
        out[r] = 2.0 * (-minimize_scalar(neg, bounds=(-2.0, 12.0), method="bounded").fun - ll0)
    return float(out.mean())


_mc = _mc_lrt(50, 3.0, 2.0)
_analytic_ref = expected_lrt(50, float(kl_loc[int(np.argmin(np.abs(mu_axis - 2.0))),
                                              int(np.argmin(np.abs(b_axis_loc - 3.0)))]))

rows = [
    ["per-gene 2*KL monotone in b (mu=1.5)", mono_b],
    ["per-gene 2*KL monotone in mu (b=2)", mono_mu],
    ["set LRT monotone in m (b=2, mu=1.5)", mono_m],
    ["reference f(m=50, b=3, mu=2) ~ 46", f"{_ref:.1f}"],
    ["LRT=25 min-b, small set m=50 (mu=1.5)", f"{_bmin_small:.2f}"],
    ["LRT=25 min-b, large set m=300 (mu=1.5)", f"{_bmin_large:.2f}"],
    ["  -> large reaches LRT=25 at smaller b", _bmin_large < _bmin_small],
    ["per-gene 2*KL loc (b=2, mu=2)", f"{_loc_at2:.3f}"],
    ["per-gene 2*KL scale (b=2, sigma=2)", f"{_sca_at2:.3f}"],
    ["  -> scale weaker per gene at matched E[z^2]=5", _scale_harder],
    ["analytic f(m=50, b=3, mu=2)", f"{_analytic_ref:.2f}"],
    ["Monte-Carlo LRT, same point (n=4000)", f"{_mc:.2f}"],
    ["  -> analytic matches MC (abs diff < 1)", abs(_analytic_ref - _mc) < 1.0],
]
print(_md(rows, ["check", "value"]))
```

## Takeaways

- **Set size is just a multiplier.** $f = 2m\,\mathrm{KL} + c$ is exactly linear in the
  set size $m$, so the per-gene contribution $2\,\mathrm{KL}$ is the whole landscape and
  size scales it. The dashed iso-detectability lines read off the tradeoff directly: a
  large set ($m = 400$) is just-detectable ($\mathrm{LRT} \approx 25$) at a per-gene signal
  $\sim$8$\times$ weaker than a small set ($m = 50$) needs.
- **Scale is weaker per gene than location.** At a matched per-gene energy and enrichment,
  the location contribution exceeds the scale one, because sign-agnostic variance inflation
  is weaker, symmetric evidence than a directed mean shift - so the scale panel's iso-size
  lines sit higher on the $\sigma$ axis.
- **The landscape is the in-set-only reduction, in closed form.** Fixing $b_0 = -2$
  makes the LRT depend on the $m$ in-set genes alone, and taking its expectation collapses
  to $2m\,\mathrm{KL}(q_\ast \Vert q_0) + c$ - one 1-D quadrature per grid point, no
  simulation. The Monte-Carlo row in the sanity table confirms the closed form.

Each figure legend defines its axes and the statistic (per-gene expected-LRT contribution
$2\,\mathrm{KL}$; set-level $f = 2m\,\mathrm{KL} + 1$; $b_0 = -2$ fixed, in-set genes only).
Everything is exact quadrature - no random draws, no cache - so re-rendering reproduces the
figures bit-for-bit.
```
